1.synthesis: The Passion narrative opens with a double frame: the chief priests and scribes seeking to arrest Jesus by stealth and kill him (avoiding the feast, lest there be an uproar — vv.1–2), and a woman in Bethany breaking an alabaster flask of costly ointment over Jesus's head (vv.3–9). The two scenes are the Markan sandwich technique applied to the Passion's opening: the anointing interprets the conspiracy. Those present object to the "waste" of three hundred denarii; Jesus defends her — she has anointed his body beforehand for burial, and wherever the gospel is proclaimed she will be remembered (vv.6–9). Then Judas goes to the chief priests to betray Jesus, and they promise him money — the opposite of the woman's extravagant giving (vv.10–11).
1.historical_context: "It was two days before the Passover and the Feast of Unleavened Bread" (v.1): the leaders' concern about timing is politically astute — Passover drew enormous crowds to Jerusalem (Josephus estimates in the millions, plausibly in the hundreds of thousands), creating conditions in which any unrest around a popular teacher could become volatile. The ointment is identified as nardos pistikēs — spikenard, imported from the Himalayan region — and worth three hundred denarii, approximately a year's wage for a common laborer (vv.3–5). Anointing the head was a royal and priestly gesture; anointing the body was a burial preparation. Simon the leper's house in Bethany (v.3) is otherwise unattested; the woman is unnamed in Mark (contrast John 12:3 where she is Mary of Bethany).
1.literary_structure: The structural sandwich — conspiracy (vv.1–2) / anointing (vv.3–9) / betrayal (vv.10–11) — frames the Passion's opening with a double contrast: the religious leaders want Jesus dead; the unnamed woman lavishes her most valuable possession on him. Judas's betrayal (vv.10–11) follows immediately after the anointing as the negative image of the woman's gift — she gives everything for Jesus; Judas sells Jesus for money. Jesus's promise in v.9 — that she will be remembered wherever the gospel is proclaimed — is fulfilled in the very act of reading this account, making the reader a participant in that remembrance. The anointing "beforehand for burial" (v.8) is the only anointing Jesus will receive; the women who come on Easter morning to anoint him find the tomb empty (16:1).
1.theological_application: "She has done what she could; she has anointed my body beforehand for burial" (v.8). Jesus's defense of the woman against the criticism of waste is a theology of extravagant devotion: discipleship that calculates cost-efficiency is not yet at the level of love. The indignant bystanders appeal to the poor (v.5 — a genuinely important concern); Jesus acknowledges the poor will always be present but the present moment with him is unique and unrepeatable. This does not undermine care for the poor but establishes that some expressions of worship exceed the category of efficient resource allocation. The woman's act is the inverse of Judas's: she breaks open her most valuable possession; he sells the most valuable person.
1.christology: The woman anoints Jesus as both king (head-anointing = royal installation: 1 Samuel 10:1; 1 Kings 1:39) and burial (body-preparation). Both meanings apply: he is the Messiah-King going to his death. The word "Christ" (Christos) means "the Anointed One" — in the very act of being anointed for burial, the woman performs what the title declares. That Jesus says she has anointed him for burial shows he accepts the anointing in full awareness of what lies ahead: the Passion is not something that happens to him but something he walks into with open eyes and kingly dignity.

12.synthesis: On the first day of Unleavened Bread, when the Passover lamb is sacrificed, the disciples ask where Jesus wants to prepare the meal (v.12). He sends two of them with precise instructions: enter the city, a man carrying a jar of water will meet them, follow him, and say to the owner of the house he enters, "The Teacher asks: where is my guest room where I may eat the Passover with my disciples?" (vv.13–14). They find everything as he said and prepare the Passover (vv.15–16). Evening: reclining with the twelve, Jesus says, "Truly, one of you will betray me, one who is eating with me" (vv.17–18). They are sorrowful and ask one by one, "Is it I?" He says: "It is one of the twelve who is dipping bread in the dish with me. For the Son of Man goes as it is written of him, but woe to that man by whom the Son of Man is betrayed!" (vv.20–21).
12.historical_context: The Passover context is central: the meal commemorates the Exodus liberation, the blood of the Passover lamb on the doorposts, and the covenant at Sinai. Jesus's preparations involve a prearranged signal — a man carrying a water jar was unusual (women carried water jars; men used wineskins), making him easily identifiable. The upper room (anagaion — a second-floor or upstairs room) was a larger furnished space, likely already arranged by a Jerusalem supporter. Reclining (anakeimenōn) was the Passover posture, symbolizing the freedom of the liberated (slaves ate standing). The betrayal announcement at table fulfilled Psalm 41:9: "Even my close friend in whom I trusted, who ate my bread, has lifted his heel against me" — a text Mark does not cite explicitly but which the scene embodies.
12.literary_structure: The preparation scene (vv.12–16) shows Jesus's sovereign foreknowledge: the details unfold exactly as predicted, establishing that the Passion events are not happening to him but through him. The betrayal announcement (vv.17–21) is structured as disclosure-without-identification: all twelve hear it, all ask, none is identified to the group. This is not a dramatic failure of identification but a deliberate withholding — Jesus gives Judas one more moment and the table-community one more chance. The "Son of Man goes as it is written" (v.21) holds together divine necessity and human responsibility without collapsing one into the other: the betrayal fulfills Scripture and remains a moral catastrophe for the betrayer.
12.theological_application: "The Son of Man goes as it is written of him, but woe to that man by whom the Son of Man is betrayed! It would have been better for that man if he had not been born" (v.21). This verse has generated sustained theological debate about the relationship between divine predetermination and human freedom. Jesus does not say "Judas has no choice because it is written" but "it is written" and simultaneously "woe to that man" — Scripture's fulfillment does not evacuate the betrayer's responsibility. The disciples' question "Is it I?" (v.19) is each one's honest self-examination at the threshold of the Passion: none is exempt from the possibility of betrayal.
12.christology: "The Son of Man goes as it is written of him" (v.21). The death Jesus is about to undergo is not a tragedy that overtook him but a path written in the Scriptures and walked with sovereign intention. The Passover setting is integral: as the lamb was slaughtered and its blood applied for Israel's liberation, so the Son of Man goes as the definitive Passover sacrifice — a connection 1 Corinthians 5:7 makes explicit ("Christ, our Passover lamb, has been sacrificed"). His foreknowledge of the betrayer, his sovereign preparation of the room, and his own self-understanding as the Son of Man going according to Scripture all portray the one who is about to suffer as the one who is fully in command of what is happening.

22.synthesis: As they were eating, Jesus took bread, blessed and broke it, gave it to them: "Take; this is my body" (v.22). Then he took a cup, gave thanks, gave it to them, and all drank from it. He said: "This is my blood of the covenant, which is poured out for many" (vv.23–24). "Truly, I say to you, I will not drink again of the fruit of the vine until that day when I drink it new in the kingdom of God" (v.25). When they had sung a hymn, they went out to the Mount of Olives (v.26). Mark's account is concise — no discourse, no institution narrative at length — but the fourfold action (took-blessed-broke-gave) is precise: it is the same fourfold pattern of the two feeding miracles (6:41; 8:6), now applied to Jesus's own body and blood at Passover.
22.historical_context: "My blood of the covenant" (v.24) echoes Exodus 24:8, where Moses sprinkles the blood of the sacrificial animals on the people and says, "Behold the blood of the covenant that the LORD has made with you" — the Sinai covenant ratified in blood. The phrase "poured out for many" echoes Isaiah 53:12, where the Servant "poured out his soul to death" and "bore the sin of many." The Passover seder included four cups of wine; the cup Jesus takes is likely the third cup (the cup of blessing/redemption). The Hallel hymn sung at the end (v.26) would have been Psalms 115–118 — including Psalm 118:22–23 (the cornerstone Psalm he cited in the Temple) and Psalm 118:25–26 (the Hosanna cry of the entry).
22.literary_structure: The institution is set within the Passover meal and draws its meaning from that setting. Mark's brevity (compared with Luke 22:14–20 and 1 Corinthians 11:23–26) is characteristic: no explanatory command to "do this in remembrance," no extended theology — simply the action and the two identification statements. The eschatological vow of abstinence (v.25) frames the Supper within the kingdom-horizon: this meal is itself a foretaste of the messianic banquet, and Jesus will not drink again until the kingdom arrives in its fullness. The departure to Gethsemane (v.26) immediately follows the singing of Psalm 118 — the crowd's Hosanna Psalm, now sung at the threshold of the Passion.
22.theological_application: "This is my blood of the covenant, which is poured out for many" (v.24). The Supper is simultaneously the New Covenant's inauguration meal (Jeremiah 31:31–34) and the Passover's fulfillment: the covenant ratified not by the blood of animals (Exodus 24) but by the blood of the Son. "For many" (hyper pollōn) is the ransom-language of 10:45 — the Son of Man gives his life as a ransom for many. The debate about the precise mode of presence (Roman Catholic transubstantiation, Lutheran consubstantiation, Reformed spiritual presence, memorial) post-dates Mark's account; what is not debated is that Jesus here identifies the bread with his body and the cup with his covenant blood poured out for many — a claim requiring either literal truth or blasphemous presumption.
22.christology: The fourfold action (took-blessed-broke-gave) has been performed twice before in Mark over physical bread and fish for hungry crowds. Now performed over bread and a cup at Passover, it transforms the feeding miracle's logic: the bread he gives to be eaten is now identified as his body; the cup he gives to be drunk is his blood. The one who fed the multitudes with physical bread now offers himself as the bread and the cup. The Passover covenant-blood context (Exodus 24:8), the Servant's pouring-out language (Isaiah 53:12), and the kingdom-horizon vow all converge: Jesus at the Supper table is simultaneously priest (offering), sacrifice (offered), and host (distributing).

27.synthesis: On the way to the Mount of Olives Jesus says: "You will all fall away, for it is written, 'I will strike the shepherd, and the sheep will be scattered'" (v.27, citing Zechariah 13:7). "But after I am raised up, I will go before you to Galilee" (v.28). Peter protests: "Even though they all fall away, I will not" (v.29). Jesus: "Truly I tell you, this very night, before the rooster crows twice, you will deny me three times" (v.30). Peter insists vehemently that he would die rather than deny — and all say the same (v.31). The prediction of universal scattering is matched immediately by the promise of post-resurrection regathering — and Peter's insistence stands against Jesus's precise counter-prophecy.
27.historical_context: Zechariah 13:7 is a divine oracle in which YHWH commands striking the shepherd so that the sheep scatter — a text of divine judgment against a shepherd figure that contextually addresses a time of testing and purification for Israel. Jesus applies it to his own arrest: the disciples' flight is not moral failure alone but fulfillment of a prophetic pattern. Mark's version specifies "before the rooster crows twice" (v.30) — a detail unique to Mark (Matthew and Luke have "before the rooster crows"), matching the two-fold crowing that punctuates Peter's denials at 14:68 and 14:72. The Galilean promise (v.28) points to the resurrection appearance commissioned there — confirmed at 16:7 by the angel's specific recall of this saying.
27.literary_structure: The Zechariah citation frames the disciples' failure as prophetically anticipated — their scattering is not a surprise to Jesus or to God. The Galilee promise (v.28) is the hinge: even in the prediction of failure, Jesus inserts the post-resurrection regathering. This structure — scattering promised / regathering promised / Peter's vehement denial of scattering — creates the dramatic irony that shapes the Passion narrative: the reader knows what Peter does not yet know about himself. Peter's vehemence (v.31: perissōs — excessively, beyond measure) measures the gap between his self-knowledge and his actual situation, a gap the night in the courtyard will expose.
27.theological_application: "But after I am raised up, I will go before you to Galilee" (v.28). The promise is spoken to the disciples who will scatter — it does not excuse the scattering but it survives it. Jesus speaks the resurrection before the cross, the regathering before the flight. This is the shape of grace: the word that goes ahead and waits. Peter's failure to internalize his own vulnerability (v.29: "even if all fall away, not I") is not unique to him; all the disciples protest, and all will scatter. The promise of v.28 is addressed to that actual company — scattered, failed, ashamed — and is the basis on which they can be restored.
27.christology: "I will strike the shepherd, and the sheep will be scattered" (Zechariah 13:7). In Zechariah, the striking is by YHWH's command. Jesus cites this of himself — he is the shepherd who will be struck by divine judgment, and the disciples are the sheep who will scatter. This is an implicit identification with the one against whom the divine sword is raised in Zechariah 13: he is not merely a teacher whose followers flee but the shepherd whose death is the divinely ordained judgment that opens the way to purification and regathering. The Galilee promise (v.28) confirms that the struck shepherd rises — the divine striking is not elimination but the path through which the shepherd goes before his flock.

32.synthesis: They come to Gethsemane; Jesus tells most disciples to sit while he prays, taking Peter, James, and John further (vv.32–33). He begins to be greatly distressed and troubled — "My soul is very sorrowful, even to death; remain here and watch" (v.34). Going a little further, he fell on the ground and prayed: "Abba, Father, all things are possible for you. Remove this cup from me. Yet not what I will, but what you will" (vv.35–36). He returns to find them sleeping and addresses Peter: "Simon, are you asleep? Could you not watch one hour?" (v.37). Watch and pray not to enter into temptation — the spirit is willing but the flesh is weak (v.38). Three times this pattern repeats; at the end: "The hour has come. The Son of Man is betrayed into the hands of sinners. Rise, let us be going; see, my betrayer is at hand" (vv.41–42).
32.historical_context: Gethsemane (gat shemanim — oil press) was an olive grove at the foot of the Mount of Olives, a regular retreat location for Jesus and the disciples (John 18:2: Judas knew the place). The strong emotional language — ekthambeisthai (greatly distressed, a uniquely intense Mark vocabulary: used also at 9:15 for the crowd's awe and 16:5–6 for the women's alarm) and adēmonein (troubled, heavy with anguish) — is the most psychologically intense portrait of Jesus in the Synoptics. The address "Abba, Father" (v.36) is unique to Mark (Matthew and Luke render it in Greek only); it is the Aramaic intimate address for father, reflecting the authentic prayer-language of the historical Jesus. Paul uses it as the Spirit's cry in the believing heart (Romans 8:15; Galatians 4:6).
32.literary_structure: The three-fold prayer (each with a return to sleeping disciples) mirrors the three denials of Peter (14:66–72) and the three Passion predictions (8:31; 9:31; 10:33): Mark's three-part structures organize the Passion's central episodes. The contrast between Jesus's watching and the disciples' sleeping answers the Olivet Discourse's closing command (13:37: "Stay awake") — they fail in the Garden precisely what the discourse commanded. The address to Peter by his former name "Simon" (v.37) — not his apostolic name Peter/Rock — suggests the pre-confession Simon who is still in process of becoming what Jesus named him.
32.theological_application: "Not what I will, but what you will" (v.36). The prayer is not a resignation to the inevitable but a genuine petition followed by a voluntary alignment of the Son's will with the Father's. The cup that Jesus prays to have removed is the cup of God's wrath poured out on sin — in OT usage, the cup of divine judgment (Isaiah 51:17; Jeremiah 25:15; Psalm 75:8). The request to have it removed is not weakness but honest prayer: Jesus brings his genuine desire to the Father rather than bypassing prayer's personal reality. The "yet not what I will" is the creaturely submission to the Creator's will that Adam and all his descendants have refused — the last Adam now offers it perfectly.
32.christology: "Abba, Father, all things are possible for you. Remove this cup from me. Yet not what I will, but what you will" (v.36). This prayer is the Gethsemane christological core: the eternal Son, genuinely incarnate, prays to the Father with genuine desire ("remove this cup") and genuine submission ("not what I will"). Docetism is excluded by the real anguish; a Nestorian split is excluded by the personal unity of the "I" who wills and submits. The Reformed tradition reads this as the human nature's will in authentic alignment with the divine will — Chalcedonian two wills in one person. That the Son in his hour of deepest testing calls God "Abba" — the intimate address — is the deepest revelation of their relationship. He goes to the cross not coerced but as the Son who loves the Father and will not circumvent the Father's way of salvation.

43.pericope_label: The Betrayal and Arrest of Jesus
43.synthesis: While Jesus is still speaking, Judas arrives with a crowd bearing swords and clubs, sent from the chief priests, scribes, and elders (v.43). The prearranged signal: "The one I will kiss is the man. Seize him and lead him away safely" (v.44). Judas comes up, says "Rabbi!" and kisses him with the emphatic kataphileō (v.45). They lay hands on him and seize him. A bystander draws a sword and cuts off the ear of the high priest's servant (vv.46–47). Jesus: "Have you come out against a robber with swords and clubs? Day after day I was with you in the Temple teaching, and you did not seize me. But let the Scriptures be fulfilled" (vv.48–49). All forsake him and flee — including a young man who slips away leaving only the linen cloth he was wrapped in (vv.50–52).
43.historical_context: A kiss (philēma) was a normal greeting between rabbi and student; Judas uses the emphatic compound kataphilein (to kiss earnestly, repeatedly) as identification signal — an act of profound irony. "The high priest's servant" (v.47) is unnamed in Mark; John names him Malchus (John 18:10) and names the swordsman Peter (John 18:11). The crowd armed with swords and clubs was likely Temple police rather than Roman soldiers — a nighttime arrest arranged to avoid crowd reaction. The young man in the linen cloth (vv.51–52) is unique to Mark, unidentified; he has been variously identified as Mark himself, a follower living nearby, or a literary detail echoing Amos 2:16. His nakedness fleeing prefigures the burial cloth left behind in the empty tomb (16:5).
43.literary_structure: "And immediately, while he was still speaking, Judas came" (v.43) — Mark's characteristic euthys (immediately) at the Passion's most violent hinge. The disciples' scattering (v.50: "all forsook him and fled") fulfills the Zechariah citation of v.27 within the same chapter — the prediction and its fulfillment are compressed into a single evening. Jesus's rebuke of the crowd's stealth ("day after day I was with you in the Temple... you did not seize me") is not a complaint but an observation about the nature of what is happening: they come in darkness with weapons against one who taught openly in daylight — which is itself the darkness that the Gospel of John makes explicit (John 3:19).
43.theological_application: "But let the Scriptures be fulfilled" (v.49). Jesus interprets his arrest not as the triumph of his enemies but as the completion of a scriptural trajectory — the specific scriptures include Isaiah 53 (the Servant handed over), Psalm 22 (the righteous sufferer abandoned), and Zechariah 13:7 (the shepherd struck). His refusal to resist the arrest and his explanation in terms of Scripture's fulfillment frame the arrest as theologically intentional: God is accomplishing his purpose through these events. The disciples' flight is not the disciples' story ending but the beginning of the story that the resurrection will reverse.
43.christology: Judas greets Jesus as "Rabbi" and kisses him — the intimacy of discipleship weaponized as betrayal. The one who is addressed as Teacher by the one who sells him continues to call himself (through the Zechariah citation in v.27 and the "let the Scriptures be fulfilled" of v.49) the shepherd who is struck, the suffering servant, the one going as it is written. He accepts the arrest — no resistance, no angels called (contrast his statement about twelve legions of angels in Matthew 26:53) — because the cup the Father has given him he will drink. The arrest is not the end of Jesus's authority but the exercise of it in a mode the disciples cannot yet understand: the power of the Son of God displayed through voluntary submission to injustice.

53.synthesis: Jesus is led to the high priest, where all the chief priests, elders, and scribes have assembled. Peter follows at a distance and enters the high priest's courtyard (vv.53–54). The council seeks testimony against Jesus to justify a death sentence; many give false testimony but none agrees (vv.55–56). Some testify: "We heard him say, 'I will destroy this temple made with hands, and in three days I will build another, not made with hands'" — but even so their testimony doesn't agree (vv.57–59). The high priest asks directly: "Are you the Christ, the Son of the Blessed?" Jesus: "I am, and you will see the Son of Man seated at the right hand of Power, and coming with the clouds of heaven" (vv.60–62). The high priest tears his garments: "Blasphemy!" They condemn him as deserving death. Spitting, blindfolding, striking, mocking: "Prophesy!" (vv.63–65).
53.historical_context: The Sanhedrin night-trial has been questioned on procedural grounds by both ancient Jewish sources (the Mishnah Sanhedrin tractate forbids capital trials at night and requires a day's delay between conviction and sentencing) and modern scholars; the procedural irregularities themselves may be part of Mark's portrait of injustice. The high priest tearing his garments (v.63) was the prescribed response to heard blasphemy in some traditions (though defined blasphemy was precisely naming the divine Name, not claiming to be the Messiah). The Temple-saying (vv.57–59) distorts a genuine dominical saying — Jesus had spoken of the Temple's destruction (13:2) and of raising a destroyed Temple in three days (John 2:19). The high priest's question combining "the Christ" and "Son of the Blessed" (a circumlocution for "Son of God") is the first formal interrogation of Jesus's identity in the Gospel.
53.literary_structure: Mark interweaves the trial scene and Peter's denial by placing Peter in the courtyard below (v.54) — the reader holds both simultaneously. The trial's structural climax is the high priest's question and Jesus's answer: "I am" (egō eimi) — the same phrase the false christs claim in 13:6, but here spoken truly. Jesus's answer combines Psalm 110:1 (seated at the right hand) and Daniel 7:13 (coming with the clouds) — both texts he had cited in the Temple controversies of ch. 12, now applied to his own future exaltation in the hearing of those who are about to condemn him. The mockery (v.65: "Prophesy!" while blindfolded) is ironic: as they speak, the prophecy is being fulfilled in the courtyard below.
53.theological_application: "You will see the Son of Man seated at the right hand of Power, and coming with the clouds of heaven" (v.62). Jesus does not defend himself against the false testimony (he could have corrected the misquotation) but waits for the question that matters. When it comes — "Are you the Christ, the Son of the Blessed?" — he answers it fully and adds the eschatological disclosure: the one being condemned in this room will be seen enthroned and coming in judgment. The high priest's verdict ("blasphemy") is the wrong verdict: the claim is true. Jesus speaks his most complete self-disclosure to the people most committed to rejecting it.
53.christology: "I am" (v.62) is Jesus's only unambiguous self-identification as the Christ in Mark's Gospel — and he says it at the moment when it will cost him his life. The combination of Psalm 110:1 and Daniel 7:13 defines his identity: he is simultaneously the Lord whom David acknowledged (Ps 110) and the Son of Man who receives the kingdom from the Ancient of Days (Dan 7). The high priest hears blasphemy; the Gospel reader hears the fullest statement of who Jesus is. His death follows from this confession: the charge of blasphemy, the trial, the crucifixion are the consequence of his truthful answer. He dies for who he is.

66.synthesis: As Peter warms himself below in the courtyard, a servant girl of the high priest approaches: "You also were with the Nazarene, Jesus" (vv.66–67). He denies and distances himself. The girl tells the bystanders: "This man is one of them" — he denies again (vv.69–70). Shortly after, bystanders press: "Certainly you are one of them, for you are a Galilean" (v.70). Peter begins to invoke a curse on himself and to swear: "I do not know this man of whom you speak" (v.71). Immediately the rooster crows a second time. Peter remembers: Jesus had said to him "Before the rooster crows twice, you will deny me three times." He broke down and wept (v.72).
66.historical_context: The three-fold denial structure echoes — and inverts — Peter's three-fold declaration that he would die rather than deny (vv.29–31). The Galilean accent accusation (v.70) reflects a real sociolinguistic fact: Galilean Aramaic was recognizably distinct from Judean Aramaic in consonantal pronunciation, particularly the treatment of gutturals. Peter's invoking of a curse (anathematizein) and swearing is an escalation: to confirm his denial with an oath and a self-curse if the oath is false. The double rooster-crow (v.72: "a second time") is unique to Mark — Matthew, Luke, and John have only a single crowing — and makes the fulfillment of Jesus's precise prediction (v.30: "before the rooster crows twice") explicit.
66.literary_structure: The interweaving of the council trial (vv.55–65) and the courtyard denial (vv.54, 66–72) is Mark's most sustained Markan sandwich. As Jesus answers the high priest's question with the full truth of his identity, Peter answers the servant girl's question with the full denial of his. The juxtaposition is devastating: Jesus is condemned for confessing who he is; Peter escapes immediate danger by denying who he is and who Jesus is. "He broke down and wept" (v.72) is the last appearance of Peter in Mark's Gospel until the angel's message singles him out by name at the empty tomb (16:7): the one who denied is the one specifically named for the restoration.
66.theological_application: Peter's weeping at the rooster-crow is the moment of recognition — not yet repentance, but the sorrow that precedes it. He "remembered the word that Jesus had spoken" (v.72): even in the moment of failure, the words of Jesus remain the criterion of truth and the instrument of self-knowledge. The contrast between Peter's weeping and Judas's later response (Matthew 27:3–5) is instructive: both are confronted with their guilt, but Peter weeps in a sorrow that proves to be life-giving (restored in Galilee), while Judas's remorse (metamelomai — regret rather than metanoia) ends in death. The difference is not the gravity of the sin but the direction the sorrow turns.
66.christology: Peter denies "this man" — using an expression of contemptuous distance for the one he had confessed as "the Christ" (8:29). At the trial upstairs, Jesus is confessing his identity with his life; in the courtyard, Peter is denying it to save his. The double rooster-crow fulfills Jesus's precise prophecy of vv.30–31 — at the moment of Peter's deepest failure, the accuracy of Jesus's foreknowledge is confirmed. The restoration promised in v.28 ("I will go before you to Galilee") will apply to Peter — and the angel's specific mention of his name (16:7) is the Risen Christ's personal address to the one whose denial is now on record. Grace does not minimize the denial; it names the denier and invites him anyway.
